headline: Momentum crowding enters hyperbolic-decay regime post-2015; FinBERT entry-suppression and VIX-velocity HMM regimes show the strongest recent Sharpe lifts for mid-frequency equity books

themes.theme: Hyperbolic Momentum-Alpha Decay & Crowding as Crash Signal
themes.summary: An arXiv paper (2512.11913, submitted Dec 2025, since withdrawn for further empirical validation — treat directionally) derives α(t) = K/(1+λt) for mechanical-factor alpha, achieving R²=0.65 vs 0.51 for linear decay. Post-2015 factor-ETF AUM growth caused observed remaining momentum alpha (0.15) to fall ~50% below model prediction (0.30), with crowding-growth correlation ρ=−0.63. The counter-intuitive finding: using crowding as a factor-selection signal produces Sharpe 0.22 vs 0.39 for plain factor-momentum — crowding predicts crash incidence (0.38× lower for crowded momentum, 1.7–1.8× higher for crowded reversal), not future alpha. Practitioner commentary via Quantocracy/Alpha Architect ('When Everyone Trades the Same Factor Playbook', May 2026) echoes the theme: crowding is a tail-risk overlay, not an entry/exit lever.
themes.implication: fits existing: momentum-trader — add a factor-ETF-flow crowding metric (e.g. momentum-ETF AUM growth rate) as a position-size scalar; halve momentum exposure when crowding signal is elevated rather than exiting outright

themes.theme: VIX Change Velocity as Primary HMM Regime Input
themes.summary: A practitioner backtest spanning Nov 2004–Jan 2026 (21.2 years) implemented a 2-state Hidden Markov Model using daily VIX *changes* rather than absolute VIX levels. Low-volatility regimes averaged 25 trading days; high-volatility regimes averaged only 8.5 days. Tactical allocation — equities in low-vol, rotate to TLT/GLD in high-vol — yielded 19.41% CAGR, Sharpe 1.22, max drawdown 19.54% vs SPY's 55.19% drawdown and 10.80% CAGR over the same window. The counter-intuitive result: VIX at 22 vs 18 is less predictive than whether VIX is accelerating; the velocity signal fires regime transitions ~3 days earlier than threshold-crossing rules.
themes.implication: fits existing: macro-aligned — replace or augment static yield-curve/DXY threshold logic with an HMM trained on daily ΔVIX; the existing regime structure (bullish/bearish) maps cleanly onto 2-state HMM outputs

themes.theme: FinBERT Negative-Sentiment Gate: Entry Suppression > Signal Generation
themes.summary: A ComSIA 2026 paper (arXiv 2601.19504, Jan 2026) backtested 100 S&P 500 stocks from Jan 2023–Jan 2025 and lifted Sharpe from 0.48 (RSI+MACD baseline) to 1.68 (+250%) by adding a single FinBERT daily-aggregate gate: block any new long entry when the aggregated news-sentiment score falls below −0.70. Total return 135.49% vs 53.18% for SPY. The mechanism is purely suppressive — it fires on roughly 10–15% of trading days and removes the worst-timing entries rather than generating new ones. Caveat: 24-month backtest window is short; hedge-fund review (arXiv 2605.05211, May 2026) notes FinBERT signals degrade under distribution shift and warns of data-leakage and illiquidity bias in most published sentiment-alpha results.
themes.implication: fits existing: news-reactive — implement a daily FinBERT pre-filter; suppress all new position entries on days where the rolling 24-hour sentiment aggregate is < −0.70, independent of the primary signal strength

themes.summary: A May 2026 Quantpedia backtest of a dual-horizon GTAA strategy on a 9-ETF universe (SPY, QQQ, EFA, EEM, GLD, USO, IEF, SHY, UUP) achieved Sharpe ~0.9 and Calmar ~0.9 with lower max drawdown than an equal-weight benchmark. The signal averages a 10-week and 25-week rate-of-change, selects the top 3 ETFs per component (50/50 weight), and applies an absolute-momentum filter that excludes any ETF with negative composite momentum. Weekly rebalance on Wednesdays. The counter-intuitive element: neither the 10-week nor 25-week signal is superior in isolation — the benefit accrues specifically from averaging the two, which dampens whipsaw entries that single-lookback systems generate at turning points.
themes.implication: fits existing: sector-rotator and commodity-momentum — both strategies likely use a single momentum lookback; blending a 10-week and 25-week RoC composite with an absolute-momentum gate directly applies to our ETF-universe strategies without changing the asset universe or hold period

themes.theme: LLM Stock-Price Forecasting: Pervasive Data Leakage and Illiquidity Bias in Published Results
themes.summary: A hedge-fund-perspective review accepted at IEEE AI 2026 (arXiv 2605.05211, Apr 2026) surveyed LLM-for-forecasting literature and identified four recurring failure modes: data leakage via look-ahead in sentiment labelling, horizon mismatch (daily signal applied to intraday data), illiquidity bias (Sharpe reflects theoretical fills at midpoint not executable at size), and sentiment fragility (FinBERT degrades under distribution shift on novel event types). The review found that in stress-tested, out-of-sample conditions — split by market *regime* rather than calendar date — the majority of published LLM Sharpe ratios do not survive. The practical counter-implication: the Sharpe 1.68 in Theme 3 should be validated with a regime-stratified OOS split before live deployment.
themes.implication: fits existing: news-reactive — before deploying the FinBERT gate (Theme 3), hold out a bear-market or high-volatility period as an OOS validation set; do not rely on calendar-date train/test splits to confirm the signal survives regime change

body_md:
## Momentum crowding enters hyperbolic-decay regime post-2015; FinBERT entry-suppression and VIX-velocity HMM regimes show the strongest recent Sharpe lifts for mid-frequency equity books

### Hyperbolic Momentum-Alpha Decay & Crowding as Crash Signal

An arXiv paper (2512.11913, submitted Dec 2025, since withdrawn for further empirical validation — treat directionally) derives α(t) = K/(1+λt) for mechanical-factor alpha, achieving R²=0.65 vs 0.51 for linear decay. Post-2015 factor-ETF AUM growth caused observed remaining momentum alpha (0.15) to fall ~50% below model prediction (0.30), with crowding-growth correlation ρ=−0.63. The counter-intuitive finding: using crowding as a factor-selection signal produces Sharpe 0.22 vs 0.39 for plain factor-momentum — crowding predicts crash incidence (0.38× lower for crowded momentum, 1.7–1.8× higher for crowded reversal), not future alpha. Practitioner commentary via Quantocracy/Alpha Architect ('When Everyone Trades the Same Factor Playbook', May 2026) echoes the theme: crowding is a tail-risk overlay, not an entry/exit lever.

**Implication:** fits existing: momentum-trader — add a factor-ETF-flow crowding metric (e.g. momentum-ETF AUM growth rate) as a position-size scalar; halve momentum exposure when crowding signal is elevated rather than exiting outright

### VIX Change Velocity as Primary HMM Regime Input

A practitioner backtest spanning Nov 2004–Jan 2026 (21.2 years) implemented a 2-state Hidden Markov Model using daily VIX *changes* rather than absolute VIX levels. Low-volatility regimes averaged 25 trading days; high-volatility regimes averaged only 8.5 days. Tactical allocation — equities in low-vol, rotate to TLT/GLD in high-vol — yielded 19.41% CAGR, Sharpe 1.22, max drawdown 19.54% vs SPY's 55.19% drawdown and 10.80% CAGR over the same window. The counter-intuitive result: VIX at 22 vs 18 is less predictive than whether VIX is accelerating; the velocity signal fires regime transitions ~3 days earlier than threshold-crossing rules.

**Implication:** fits existing: macro-aligned — replace or augment static yield-curve/DXY threshold logic with an HMM trained on daily ΔVIX; the existing regime structure (bullish/bearish) maps cleanly onto 2-state HMM outputs

### FinBERT Negative-Sentiment Gate: Entry Suppression > Signal Generation

A ComSIA 2026 paper (arXiv 2601.19504, Jan 2026) backtested 100 S&P 500 stocks from Jan 2023–Jan 2025 and lifted Sharpe from 0.48 (RSI+MACD baseline) to 1.68 (+250%) by adding a single FinBERT daily-aggregate gate: block any new long entry when the aggregated news-sentiment score falls below −0.70. Total return 135.49% vs 53.18% for SPY. The mechanism is purely suppressive — it fires on roughly 10–15% of trading days and removes the worst-timing entries rather than generating new ones. Caveat: 24-month backtest window is short; hedge-fund review (arXiv 2605.05211, May 2026) notes FinBERT signals degrade under distribution shift and warns of data-leakage and illiquidity bias in most published sentiment-alpha results.

**Implication:** fits existing: news-reactive — implement a daily FinBERT pre-filter; suppress all new position entries on days where the rolling 24-hour sentiment aggregate is < −0.70, independent of the primary signal strength

### Multi-Horizon ETF Momentum Blend (10-week + 25-week RoC)

A May 2026 Quantpedia backtest of a dual-horizon GTAA strategy on a 9-ETF universe (SPY, QQQ, EFA, EEM, GLD, USO, IEF, SHY, UUP) achieved Sharpe ~0.9 and Calmar ~0.9 with lower max drawdown than an equal-weight benchmark. The signal averages a 10-week and 25-week rate-of-change, selects the top 3 ETFs per component (50/50 weight), and applies an absolute-momentum filter that excludes any ETF with negative composite momentum. Weekly rebalance on Wednesdays. The counter-intuitive element: neither the 10-week nor 25-week signal is superior in isolation — the benefit accrues specifically from averaging the two, which dampens whipsaw entries that single-lookback systems generate at turning points.

**Implication:** fits existing: sector-rotator and commodity-momentum — both strategies likely use a single momentum lookback; blending a 10-week and 25-week RoC composite with an absolute-momentum gate directly applies to our ETF-universe strategies without changing the asset universe or hold period

### LLM Stock-Price Forecasting: Pervasive Data Leakage and Illiquidity Bias in Published Results

A hedge-fund-perspective review accepted at IEEE AI 2026 (arXiv 2605.05211, Apr 2026) surveyed LLM-for-forecasting literature and identified four recurring failure modes: data leakage via look-ahead in sentiment labelling, horizon mismatch (daily signal applied to intraday data), illiquidity bias (Sharpe reflects theoretical fills at midpoint not executable at size), and sentiment fragility (FinBERT degrades under distribution shift on novel event types). The review found that in stress-tested, out-of-sample conditions — split by market *regime* rather than calendar date — the majority of published LLM Sharpe ratios do not survive. The practical counter-implication: the Sharpe 1.68 in Theme 3 should be validated with a regime-stratified OOS split before live deployment.

**Implication:** fits existing: news-reactive — before deploying the FinBERT gate (Theme 3), hold out a bear-market or high-volatility period as an OOS validation set; do not rely on calendar-date train/test splits to confirm the signal survives regime change

### Sources

- https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.11913
- https://arxiv.org/html/2601.19504v1
- https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05211
- https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23007
- https://quantpedia.com/active-dual-momentum-gtaa-strategy/?a=6080
- https://medium.com/@Splendor001/regime-based-portfolio-allocation-a-hidden-markov-model-approach-to-tactical-asset-rotation-4ff3fdf6f9f8
- https://quantocracy.com/
- https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5023380
- https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5023370
